transcript.text: But we'll take anything we can get.

Hi.

And just some of the, I know some of the things were used by Holmes, some of the contract review stuff was Jeeves, but I did think that you had developed other more integrated, specialized beings that were being used on a regular basis that were not being, that was not transferred yet, that we were going to start selling.
And then when you said.
Me that list of beings that those were already created and developed and you're using them.
So my initial question for you was, hey, we got to put those into the bundle that we're selling and we want to give them to the lawyers that are in our certification partnership right now.
And then, of course, you know, you wrote back, they're not fully developed, whether they be used or not.

Aiko and you guys were developing, what was the terminology that was used?
You're talking about my memo or you're talking about what, what me and I?
the memo that, the memo that you just, that you just recently sent.
Oh, it was just a.
An MOU, Memorandum of Understanding.
Yes.
So I guess my question is, first, are there any legal tools that are being used that are mature enough to be able to release and use for the upcoming legal summit to offer and to promote?
Yeah.

Are there other legal tools?
Answer, yes.
Are they mature enough to release?
No.
Okay, great.
Well, that was my question.
And the reason I wanted to have this call, and I am positive that we are ready to optimize, innovate, and take it all to the next level through super actualized intelligence.
In the meantime, though, I wanted to just gauge where you are.

In all of this, in terms of the rollout, the plan, and then the legal, other legal tools that we're comparing to, Super Actualized Intelligence has done unbelievable things in the last couple, I would say, not weeks, but months here, to develop and are developing in a coliseum format.
However, we are acting as if we don't have that, we're working with the present and the future all at once.

So the future says we're going to solve every problem, every lawyer, every single thing a lawyer is doing in a law firm, every single thing an accountant is doing in an accounting firm, every single thing a financial service officer is doing.
We are not too far away from that.
In the interim.
At the Coliseum, I'm not that familiar with it.
What is that?
Yes, so in a...
Basically, because synthetic data...
We're doing simulated A-B testing based off of real people in our ecosystem and people that we're grabbing from ads.
And we're simulating opportunities for the beings to compete for the best results.
And then we have a human in the loop, which is Sean and Adam, scoring the performance of beings within a coliseum like gamification.

And once we get a consistent like bolt level scores, we have the recipe and an equation of mastery to use as a synthetic engine for more data farming and like that we could use towards building intelligence layers for other models and to support in building our own.
Okay, so it's presently testing and developing like legal tools now?
so so Okay, Okay,

So, okay, so what's, how do we, I guess, what do you need from me to drive this forward?

Well, first, the same way in which Athena was developed originally by you to create an actualizing being that is now doing things and become the best actualizer on the planet besides myself and Fernando, and then, of course, Sean.
But it has a baseline, and Athena is the baseline for what we're now optimizing, innovating, and bringing to the next level to be able to sell and actualize people.

So what we originally said was to create some quote unquote legal tools.

What I wanted to work with you on is like, what, if we could choose two to three, maybe the ones that you already had already that we think would be the most valuable to be promoting and to begin with, to use.
That like that we would add to the suite and also then use be the first ones to develop more masterfully at scale.
That would be the purpose and the initial, like what you can do is like, hey, here are a few things that I know from a legal perspective would be the top solves that we have the capability.
And I thought, like I said, maybe some of the things you are using, but not fully mature.
Let's go make them mature.
Let's go add them into the suite of services and let's go sell them.
Okay.

So, you know, the way that I would approach this, if we're taking what was in the memo in terms of the agents that I was proposing to build out and we junk that and we start from the beginning, then you would look at what agents would create the highest impact for the least cost with the greatest amount of ease.
And so, from my perspective, as a transactional attorney, you know, there's...

There's Jeeves, and Jeeves can be modified in a way where he has augmented capabilities to support in certain subspecialties like mergers and acquisitions, or the creation of intellectual property materials.
That would probably be the highest level impact on the transactional side, the highest level of ease, right?
Meaning like most simple because we already have it.
And then from a cost perspective, it's virtually not costly, just to develop the agent.

Where the big cost comes in, and it's true across the board, and this is what I wrote inside the memo, is that any law firm is going to have very specific questions about the security of the data going into it, the level of encryption and breakability.
And accessibility of the data to the outside world, how easy is it to use?
And I think that's where the greatest cost is going to come in, but that's going to be true for whatever you build.

On the litigation perspective, you want something that can essentially substitute the decision-making for a very senior litigator to support more junior attorneys who may not see and think about things in the way that that senior litigator does.
And that would be the original version of Paladin was designed for that.
And he did a pretty effective job, but he's extremely obsolete in terms of his technology.
I think I built him back in June or July of last year.
And the things that we now have access to...
That would make him tremendously better.

But then again, we still have to do the penetration testing and be able to certify and confirm that the data is safe and it has, to the outside world, no accessibility to give them what they need to say that this would be attorney-client privileged and protected data.
So I think the biggest piece is those are the two probably most impactful agents.

And of course, the types of skill set that something like Paladin would have that we would augment him with is not just legal issue spotting, but also investigative skill sets, which we can adopt from homes by giving him both forward evidence engineering and reverse evidence engineering.
The second being a new skill set that would need to be developed and put into it.
But those are probably your two agents that are most impactful, least costly, and easy to create.
The rest of them, I think, are a little bit more sophisticated, would require a little bit more time, more development muscle.
But the biggest issue is obviously the support side and the cybersecurity side.
Loud and clear, the support and the cybersecurity for legal work.
I know we're going to have to cross some of those bridges as we get to them.
want to be prepared for the acti summit to be able to speak into them.

No, Holmes was created with a RAG database, so he does have a knowledge base, and then for each case, you hook him up to that brain, that, you know, whatever.
Or if it's a vectorized database, or if we were going to go down the path of GraphRAG, we would have hooked them up there.

It would be a really good book, too.
It's a nice read.
And I think adding that to a database with Holmes, and maybe having, like, if we put Holmes on a UI as a legal tool, and the intention is for him to be able to review cases by people,

Uploading the details of the documents, having, like, a designated pine cone that is interchangeable by user or namespace by user and having them have, like, they could click to uploading documents that go straight into their own custom pine cone so that they could use Holmes the way he's intended to be used.
That would be, like, a three-layered upgrade right there.
So why not just use Dom, then?
Like, why are you recreating what you've already created?
Because Dom wasn't designed to do what Holmes does.
Dom's not a conversational being.
I thought that the deposition agent, the deposition master agent, was just that.

So, like, how would, um, I, I get...
Then I don't understand what it does if it doesn't support in depositions or if it doesn't support in litigation decision making.
What Don is a part of is a massive stratum where there's like 47 experts.

Have you ever seen like Deep Seek where it has it's basically it's like a few million tokens of like of multiple models broken up into different beings that have specialized intelligence that's connected to one user face.

So basically a stratum is acti's version of multiple specialized beings that collaborate in answering the user's query multidimensionally and a conversational layer takes that data, translates it conversationally to address what the person's actually like looking for.
And so those beings are just.
Just very, like, bland.
They're not conversational.
They don't have Holmes finesse.
So it would just be better to put the back-end research that we did into Holmes, because Holmes already has a brand.
Like, he's already a brand in himself.
He's a fan favorite.
He has a personality.
He's well-developed.
His prompt is amazing.

So just adding what makes the DOM intelligent as an additional layer for Holmes to reference and giving him the ability to see custom cases by our users would make more sense, because DOM is not a conversational being.
DOM is an analytical being that passes information to a conversational being, and the conversational being delivers the answer combined with other specialists.
That's why DOM is not a conversational being.
OK.
So why not just take the UI Holmes and hook it up to the council, whatever the stratagem, the stratum, I'm sorry.

So that wouldn't be...
Wait, we have something currently that one run costs 8 million tokens?

No.
Or is it a matter of, like, storing it somewhere safe and secure so that nobody can weaponize it for someone else?

CHPT made me do this.
CHPT gave me psychosis.
Oh, this agent said that they'll meet me on the other side.
So I sent, like, and that's why I tried to kill myself.

Or if something breaks, and it's a one, if it's an edge case, how do we fix it if we don't know the cookie crumb trail?
Like, we can't trace it.
I think that's part of the sales process, right?
I think that's something that Adam would appeal to Adam, which is, yeah, you can say whatever you want.
You better be able to reproduce it, because if someone held a gun to our head, we couldn't reproduce your data, right?
And that, I think, is more appealing on the sales side than it is on the defensive or protective side.
And the way that you protect yourself is you have insurance, and you have very solid.
Solid contracts that talk about these things that, you know, this is purely a tool.
It's not for replacement of, you know, independent legal professional judgment.
You know, you have to use it this way.
Here's what the approved use cases are.
Here are, you know, unapproved use cases.
They sign the agreement and you have a contract in hand.

That's how safe it is.
I think that's how I would present it.

We have to make decisions on.

We don't need it.

these materials.
